region_of_origin: Global (popularised in Western Europe and North America; widely served in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_00002694 covers french fries generically; salted variant is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Potato chips / crisps and similar fried potato snacks
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fried potato snack category; no dedicated code for restaurant-style salted fries. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226803007 covers french fried potatoes generically; salted variant not separately coded. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of salted fries is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a medium-sized portion as typically served at a casual dining establishment.

nutrient_source.title: Potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, as purchased — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170697)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for salted frozen french fries used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent the fried potato product with salt added. Restaurant-prepared salted fries are nutritionally comparable; minor variation in fat content is expected depending on frying oil and drain time. Values adapted to represent a standard deep-fried salted fry product.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 75 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) for french fries / fried potato products. Multiple entries for french fries in the Sydney GI Database report GI values in the range 63–75 (glucose reference = 100). A value of 75 is used as a conservative representative estimate for deep-fried salted fries. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 41.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 46.1.

hypertension.guidance: Salted fries contain approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (315 mg per 150 g serving), which contributes meaningfully to the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and avoidance of additional salt or high-sodium condiments such as the accompanying curry ketchup are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index and high carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salted fries have a high glycemic index of approximately 75 and a glycemic load of approximately 46 per 150 g serving, making them likely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, pair with low-GI foods, and monitor blood glucose response carefully.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat, saturated fat, and sodium content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salted fries provide 15 g total fat and 2.3 g saturated fat per 100 g along with moderate sodium; regular consumption in large portions may adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional small servings prepared in unsaturated oils.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted fries are deep-fried potato strips seasoned with salt, representing one of the most widely consumed snack and side-dish foods globally. The potatoes are cut into elongated strips, blanched or par-cooked, then deep-fried in vegetable oil at high temperature until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining soft and fluffy on the inside. The characteristic flavour profile combines the natural starchy sweetness of potato with the savoury sharpness of salt and the rich, slightly nutty taste imparted by the frying oil. In the context of this record, the dish is served at Mingle - Funan in Singapore accompanied by a homemade curry ketchup, reflecting a fusion of Western fast-food tradition with local Singaporean flavour preferences. Nutritionally, salted fries are energy-dense, providing approximately 312 kcal per 100 g, with a significant carbohydrate content of 41 g and fat content of 15 g per 100 g. The glycemic index is high (approximately 75), making this food a concern for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. Sodium content of approximately 210 mg per 100 g is relevant for hypertensive individuals. Salted fries are naturally gluten-free and vegan when prepared without dairy-based coatings. They are a popular snack, appetiser, and side dish across casual dining, fast-food, and restaurant settings in Singapore and worldwide.
common_user_queries: How many calories are in salted fries?
Are salted fries suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of french fries?
How much sodium is in salted fries?
What are the macros in salted fries?
Is it okay to eat fries if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic load of fries?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for salted frozen french fries (FDC ID 170697), which is a closely comparable product. Restaurant-prepared fries may differ in fat absorption and sodium depending on preparation technique and oil type. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database range for french fries. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry for salted fries was identified. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input was 'Italian' (from restaurant Mingle - Funan which serves Italian-style food); however, salted fries are a globally generic Western food and the cuisine field has been corrected to 'Western / International' to reflect the food's actual origin rather than the restaurant's cuisine category. 2. The dish is described as served with homemade curry ketchup; the nutrient values here represent the fries only and do not include the curry ketchup condiment. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this product. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this product. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; potatoes and vegetable oil contain no vitamin B12, confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; this product contains no animal-derived ingredients when prepared in vegetable oil, confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 7. GL per serving calculated as (75 × 41.0 g carbohydrate × 1.5 serving size factor) / 100 = 46.1. 8. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore. 9. Price SGD 11.00 from field survey input preserved as contextual metadata but not included in RESULT template fields.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft built. Cuisine corrected from 'Italian' to 'Western / International'. Multilingual names added for Singaporean context. USDA SR Legacy selected as nutrient source. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Ontology codes assigned as closest_parent with gap notes. Zero-value justifications added for vitamin_b12 and cholesterol. Sentinel -1 values documented for vitamin_a and vitamin_d.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency against flat aliases list — all entries confirmed present verbatim. Verified GI category threshold: GI 75 ≥ 70 → 'high' confirmed correct. Verified all hard gates. Score confirmed 8/8. Final decision set to PASS.
